values.content: You're a helpful intelligent research assistant that searches online for things related to the content of social media posts.

Your task is to take as input a social media post and find three sources that discuss similar things to that social media posts. You then return a brief summaries of each resource in JSON using this format:
{"SourceOneSummary":"", "SourceTwoSummary":"", "SourceThreeSummary":""}

Rules:
- Lean towards detailed summaries.
- If you can find new information (not present in the source content) make sure to include it.
- Ensure your summaries are at least one paragraph long.

parameters.text: What's in this image? Describe this extremely comprehensively.

values.content: You're a helpful, intelligent writing assistant that takes content & some additional research and adds unique twists and more information to it (so that it reads differently).|

Your task is to take as input a social media post and a bunch of supplied research and generate a detailed, comprehensive outline of that content.
You must also do one additional thing: make that outline unique by incorporating elements from the research, as well as your own knowledge.

Output your unique outlines in JSON using this format:
{"Outline":""}

Rules:
- Keep all of the elements from the
source content, but make it better, more comprehensive, more interesting, and/or add a twist.
- Do not repeat the content verbatim-we are not copying the source, we are improving it. Make your outline different (while ensuring you maintain the same informational content).
- Output your outline in Markdown (atx) format. Be comprehensive.

json.content:
The cold email system that finally worked 👇🏻
 
In 2024, I struggled with cold emails.
 
I sent hundreds of emails, and guess what?
 
No replies.
 
Spam filters blocked most of them.
 
The ones that got through? Completely ignored.
 
I realized the problem wasn't just spam filters, it was that my entire approach was broken.
 
Here's how I turned things around:
 
1️⃣ Fixed my deliverability first
 
I stopped blasting emails blindly and started warming up my email accounts properly. With Saleshandy, I could connect unlimited email accounts and rotate between senders automatically. Their sequence score feature showed me exactly what was wrong with my emails before I even hit send.
 
2️⃣ Built campaigns that scale
 
Using Saleshandy's automated sequences, I created multi-step campaigns with personalized follow-ups. The sub-sequences feature let me move interested prospects into different flows automatically based on their replies. No more manual sorting.
 
3️⃣ Tracked everything that mattered
 
Saleshandy's unified inbox brought all replies from multiple accounts into one place. Their AI categorized responses automatically like positive, not interested, out of office. I could finally see what was working and double down on it.
 
The results?
 
- Open rates jumped from 15% to 45%
- Reply rates went from zero to 21%
- Closed 160+ leads for my clients
 
I could manage everything from one dashboard. No more juggling between tools or paying per inbox like other platforms charge.
 
Saleshandy changed how I think about cold outreach. It's not about sending more emails. It's about sending the right emails to the right people at the right time.
 
Over to you: What's your biggest challenge with cold emails right now?

Here are seven key ways AI agents are reshaping contact center operations, driving innovation, and elevating service quality. 👇

1️⃣ Autonomous customer interaction management.

AI agents can manage complete customer journeys without human intervention, achieving high resolution rates and stronger customer satisfaction scores. By maintaining context across channels and interactions, they deliver seamless experiences that drive customer loyalty.

2️⃣ Agent augmentation and support. 

During live calls, AI agents assist human agents in real time by suggesting next-best actions and surfacing relevant information. This reduces administrative burdens, allowing agents to focus on resolving complex issues and delivering higher-quality service.

3️⃣ Predictive and proactive service.

By analyzing customer interaction patterns, AI agents anticipate needs before they arise. Proactive outreach—such as alerting customers to potential issues—boosts satisfaction and shifts service models from reactive to truly customer-centric.

4️⃣ Omnichannel orchestration.

AI agents maintain conversation context across voice, chat, email, SMS, and social media, ensuring smooth transitions between channels. This seamless orchestration reduces customer frustration and creates a consistent, positive experience.

5️⃣ Intelligent routing and prioritization.

Using sentiment analysis and intent recognition, AI agents route issues based on customer history and complexity. Smarter routing minimizes call transfers, improves first-contact resolution rates, and enhances contact center efficiency.

6️⃣ Knowledge management and information access.

AI agents can retrieve information across disconnected systems, ensuring both AI and human agents have access to up-to-date knowledge bases. Faster information retrieval means quicker resolutions and better service outcomes.

7️⃣ Analytics and continuous improvement.

AI agents gather and analyze interaction data to spot improvement opportunities. Their self-learning capabilities create a continuous feedback loop that drives ongoing optimization across contact center operations.

See how AI agents are creating a new era of service excellence, read the full article here:  https://ow.ly/W60h50Wakyk

json.content:
Thoughts on the new Emergence Capital Benchmarks from today: 

1️⃣ No surprise that AI-native companies are growing 4x faster than traditional SaaS, but the 21% higher NRR I think is a function of mass net new users kicking the tires, and likely will come back down as AI products fail to deliver on efficiency gains, or newer shiny companies pop up. While this may continue for the best companies, average AI company NRR will likely drop.

2️⃣ Somewhat skeptical of the “emergence rate” (the speed at which AI systems learn and improve from customer usage) as a “new form of defensibility” since most AI app layer companies have less technical defensibility are based on similar foundational stacks that all can improve just as quickly. 

3️⃣ 2025 vs 2024 Growth rates (From Emergence report): For top quartile, ARR growth rates are actually slower than last year’s data across the board, but CAC payback and burn multiples are WAY lower. 

📈 GTM Specific Insights:
1️⃣ Most startups (59%) are leaning into AI GTM, so Clay's third massive round in the last 12 months at $3B seems well deserved.

2️⃣ SDR/BDR roles were the only sales team roles with material net decrease (36% decrease vs 19% increase), showing AI automation for list building, account research and outreach is replacing butts in seats.

3️⃣ Revenue Forecast Confidence (65%) is tougher with more competition, longer sales cycles, and automation driving insane inbox fatigue. Demand is not even a concern. We’re seeing the other end of forecast difficulty across our portfolio where AI winners can’t forecast because they’re landing massive deals they hadn’t even planned on.

4️⃣ Everyone is getting stuck in more/longer POC’s, TTV for your product needs to be faster than ever before.

5️⃣ More referrals, less events! Don’t neglect CS over pure growth or your GDR will plummet!

As always, amazing job to the Emergence folks for putting this together. You can check out the full report here (https://lnkd.in/eWDagkiv).

json.content:
4 sales triggers that book meetings on autopilot — without guesswork 

Let’s talk about timing in sales.

Back when I was making 60–80 cold calls a day, I heard the same line over and over:

"Call me back in a couple of months."

At the time, it felt like a polite no.

But then something surprising happened...

Months later, those same prospects reached back out — without a follow-up from me.

And that month?

3 net new enterprise deals were closed, all from people who once seemed like a dead end.

That’s when it clicked:

Sales isn’t just about outreach — it’s about timing the outreach.

And that’s where sales triggers come in.

They help you identify who’s ready to buy — right now.

Here are the 4 triggers we recommend that consistently drive qualified conversations 👇

🔥 1. Companies Actively Hiring → Tool: TheirStack

When a company posts a job related to your service, they’re signalling budget + urgency.

We use TheirStack to monitor live job posts and surface companies hiring for roles like “marketing lead” or “rev ops manager.”

Why it works: You’re offering support at the exact moment they’re investing in the problem.

🔥 2. Website Visitors → Tools: Instantly.ai + RB2B

Anonymous traffic = lost opportunity.

We use RB2B and Instantly to reveal which companies are browsing your site.

Once identified, they’re enriched and dropped into automated follow-up.

Why it works: They’re already curious. You’re simply closing the loop.

🔥 3. LinkedIn Engagement → Tools: Trigify.io or Teamfluence™

Likes, comments, profile views — all warm signals.

We scrape these using Trigify, then qualify with AI inside Clay before reaching out.

Why it works: You’re contacting people who already know your face and name.

🔥 4. Job Changes → Tool: LinkedIn

New roles = new decisions.

We track role changes in our target segments and reach out with relevant case studies to help them succeed early in their new position.

Why it works: People want quick wins when they start new roles — and you can be the solution.

💡 How we automate it all:

All triggers feed into Clay, updated daily.

AI qualifies based on ICP, buying intent, and timing.

Outreach sequences are triggered only when the fit is right.

Result?

A system that runs in the background, surfaces high-intent leads, and books meetings while you focus on the work.

Sales isn’t about being pushy. It’s about showing up when it matters.

Sales triggers make that possible — and predictable.

json.content:
I once ran operations at a real estate tech startup where we couldn't afford engineers… So I automated an entire department using a $25 macro tool and a second-hand laptop.

That janky system replaced what would have been 5+ full-time employees.
This was years before ChatGPT, but it taught me something I carry with me to this day..

Operational leverage beats headcount every time. Especially in capital-intensive businesses like real estate.

Here's what most operators get wrong. They think scaling means more people.
The truth is that growing your team linearly with workload is a path to disaster. 

The most successful companies I've worked with all follow the same playbook:

1) Identify the repetitive processes first.

Look at your team’s day-to-day activities. The most expensive inefficiencies aren't in your product roadmap. They're hiding in what your best people do manually over and over again (which can lead to their burnout, and helps no one).

With one client, we found their sales team spent 3.5 hours per day just preparing for calls. We built an automated system that condensed prospect research into a 2-minute briefing, giving them back 87% of that time to focus on closing.

2) Start with lightweight automation, then layer in AI.

Don't jump straight to complex AI implementations. Begin with basic no-code automation like Zapier or n8n to connect your existing tools. Then gradually add decision-making capabilities with AI.

A multifamily analytics startup I worked with started simply by automating their outreach sequences. Once that was working, we added an AI layer to personalize each message based on what was converting manually. Close rates jumped 34%.

3) Design for adaptability, not permanence.

The biggest mistake I saw was creating brittle systems that break when conditions change. Ops heavy businesses are full of exceptions and edge cases (you just gotta make it work sometimes).

Instead, build simple, modular workflows that non-technical team members can modify. When we set up content systems for clients, we create templates with swappable components rather than fully automated black boxes. Needs change over time, and we want to build for flexibility. 

The most valuable companies I've seen aren't those with the most impressive tech stacks. They're the ones who've mastered automated leverage with simplicity, which lets them move faster than anyone else.

json.content:
Spam is a legitimate problem. The bar for quality prospecting is so freaking low.


I get 5-10 of these per day, and I read every single one. So do a lot of your prospects even though they claim they don't.

I respond to quality outreach. I am a buyer. I spend 10s of thousands a month of tools/services.

What I don't respond to is low quality messaging that uses AI for no reason in ways that don't make sense. 

People complain about spam. I also hate spam. Spam is the reason our job is hard. Deliverability is always the biggest blocker because so many companies work hard to prevent it, and end up weeding out helpful messages.

Here are the red flags 🚨 in this message: 

1. I mentor at Clay Bootcamp 🥾, but I don't work there. As much as I love my friend Nathan Lippi 🥾, I don't spend my time trying to scale his business. 

2. This person literally just had AI scrape my JD from my LinkedIn bio, but didn't contextualize how it even made sense to their offer. Don't use AI just to use AI. Use it where it makes sense.
 
3. This person offered me a service that we do exceptionally well. What makes you think that I want to outsource something that we do for our clients. We do all of own outbound because I know and built the systems to do it alongside our insanely talented team. If I didn't believe in what we do, I wouldn't be offering it as a service.
 
4. Did you just ask a business owner if pipeline is on their radar? That's like asking if I breathe air 😂. 

To be fair, this isn't even close to the worst message I've received today.

How would you fix this? I have my own ideas, but I want to hear yours.

json.content:
Let AI Qualify. Let Humans Close.

Most sales organizations today are over-relying on headcount and outdated funnels. Leads get dumped into CRMs, sales reps grind through outreach, and conversion rates remain stubbornly low.

We believe the real breakthrough lies at the top of the funnel — where AI doesn’t just assist, but leads.

We’ve reimagined the sales process for clients by letting AI take the first steps: engaging, enriching, and initiating conversations.

Flipping the Funnel: 3 Key Changes
Using our agent store, we’ve introduced three deliberate upgrades to the traditional lead generation model:

1) Proactive Conversational Bots
Instead of passive “Let us know how we can help” chat windows, we deploy AI chat interfaces that initiate the interaction.

These bots engage site visitors with intent-driven questions, qualify interest, and populate structured CRM records — without human involvement.

-Higher engagement
-Richer data capture
-Lower drop-off rates

2) Real-Time Context from Market Eye Agents
Every inbound lead is enriched instantly using our Market Eye agents, which pull live firmographics, technographics, and behavioral signals from a variety of public sources to add more context so that the right offer can be targeted

This transforms each inbound or conversational lead into a full profile — with buyer readiness indicators baked in.

3) Intelligent Outreach Agents
Our Outreach Agents then follow up using tailored sequences informed by the context above with appropriate personalization

Email, LinkedIn, or SMS — the channel is dynamic, the message is personal, and the goal is clear: drive meetings.

We track this with a simple, high-impact metric: number of meetings setup per 100 leads

And it’s consistently outperforming traditional sales outreach model by a margin.

Why This Matters Beyond the Funnel:
This isn’t just about conversion rates today.
Every interaction captured through this AI-led system becomes first-party data — structured, contextual, and ethically owned. This data is the foundation for future machine learning models that can score intent, predict close likelihood, and optimize sales motion across the board.

Sales doesn’t need more tools layered onto broken processes. It needs a new architecture — one where AI leads at the top, qualifies with intelligence, and hands off to humans only when it counts.

json.content:
Most Customer Success teams are still stuck in the check-in era.
If you want to actually scale value, Proactive Engagement is the system - and when you combine it with AI, you make it unstoppable.

Here’s the old playbook:

1. Outreach is based on time, not need.
2. Engagement is triggered by calendar events, not customer behavior.
3. And playbooks exist… but rarely get used.

That’s not engagement, that’s just activity.

So what’s next?



👉 Proactive Engagement.

It means delivering value before the customer asks,
driven by behavior signals, milestone data, and usage trends.

It’s:

 ✅ Automated
 ✅ Personalized
 ✅ Timed around customer context—not your internal cadence

It’s not just a communication strategy.
It’s a retention and growth system.



🧭 Why It Matters for CX

Customers don’t churn because you forgot to check in.
They churn because they stopped seeing progress, and no one noticed.

That’s where proactive engagement changes the game:

→ Eliminates friction before it becomes frustration

→ Creates momentum toward outcomes

→ Makes your journey map feel alive to the customer

🎯 It’s how you make CX measurable and repeatable.



📉 How It Impacts Retention

Retention isn’t saved at the renewal.
It’s earned with tiny moments of value that add up over time.

With proactive engagement, you can:

→ Trigger nudges when milestones are missed

→ Reinforce value after key feature use

→ A timely heads-up before accounts drift into “ghost” mode



🚀 How It Fuels Expansion

When engagement is behavior-driven:

→ You know exactly when a customer is ready for more

→ You can deliver personalized success stories that spark interest

→ You position CS as a growth engine, not just a support layer

Expansion happens when the customer says:

“This is working. What else can we do with it?”



🤖 How AI Amplifies This Strategy

AI doesn’t replace proactive engagement.
It amplifies it.

It helps you:

→ Detect micro-signals in real time

→ Prioritize accounts by urgency

→ Trigger engagement automatically, based on behavior, not guesswork

→ Personalize messaging across thousands of customers instantly

But AI only works if the system is in place.



🧱 4 Steps to Implement Digital Proactive Engagement

1. Define success per segment and start with the outcomes that matter

2. Identify key behavior signals: Milestone completion, usage trends, goal stagnation

3. Map signal-to-engagement playbooks: “When X happens → we do Y”

4. Automate execution: Use AI + systems to trigger the right message at the right time

This is how you stop chasing accounts…
and start scaling value proactively, consistently, and with purpose.


👇
 What’s one behavior signal your team uses to trigger engagement?

#CustomerSuccess #ProactiveEngagement #ChurnPrevention #CustomerExperience

What entrepreneurship actually looks like (spoiler: it's not more free time).

For the last 10 years, I had the same job. Day in, day out. Same responsibilities. Same structure. And while I’m grateful for the stability, I knew I wanted more.

Now, every day looks different. I get to work with incredible clients across multiple industries, building out lead generation systems, growth strategies, custom AI solutions, sales funnels, graphic design, and more.

I’m learning, evolving, and creating value in ways I never could before.

But let’s be clear...freedom doesn’t mean less work. It means more ownership.

I wake up at 5 AM. I spend 15 minutes with my cat (non-negotiable). Then it’s straight into outreach videos. I film 1 to 3 hours a day to build visibility and generate leads. It’s the engine behind my business.

This is the most structured and focused I’ve ever been and it’s paying off. In just three weeks, I’ve generated more income than I was making in my previous full-time job.

A Job Search Routine That Works
One of the biggest mistakes I see?

People are job hunting in random bursts of panic.

Here’s a calm, sustainable routine that works:

Monday – Research & Shortlist 🗓 
Pick 15–20 high-quality roles. Prioritize new ones (<7 days old).

Tuesday – Resume Tailoring 🛠 
Use AI to tailor your resume for 3–5 top roles. Focus on the top third.

Wednesday – Outreach 📬
Send 3–5 short DMs to hiring managers. Be specific, not spammy.

Thursday – Apply & Track🚀 
Apply to your shortlist. Log everything (Bloom helps here, too).

Friday – Reflect & Iterate🔁 
Did you get replies? If not, try changing the subject line or your resume headline.

Consistency beats chaos.

And this system works.

article.description: The Social Media Lead Generation Revolution: Why Traditional Methods Are DEAD and What Actually Works in 2025Listen up. If you're still treating Linke

json.content:
The cold email system that finally worked 👇🏻
 
In 2024, I struggled with cold emails.
 
I sent hundreds of emails, and guess what?
 
No replies.
 
Spam filters blocked most of them.
 
The ones that got through? Completely ignored.
 
I realized the problem wasn't just spam filters, it was that my entire approach was broken.
 
Here's how I turned things around:
 
1️⃣ Fixed my deliverability first
 
I stopped blasting emails blindly and started warming up my email accounts properly. With Saleshandy, I could connect unlimited email accounts and rotate between senders automatically. Their sequence score feature showed me exactly what was wrong with my emails before I even hit send.
 
2️⃣ Built campaigns that scale
 
Using Saleshandy's automated sequences, I created multi-step campaigns with personalized follow-ups. The sub-sequences feature let me move interested prospects into different flows automatically based on their replies. No more manual sorting.
 
3️⃣ Tracked everything that mattered
 
Saleshandy's unified inbox brought all replies from multiple accounts into one place. Their AI categorized responses automatically like positive, not interested, out of office. I could finally see what was working and double down on it.
 
The results?
 
- Open rates jumped from 15% to 45%
- Reply rates went from zero to 21%
- Closed 160+ leads for my clients
 
I could manage everything from one dashboard. No more juggling between tools or paying per inbox like other platforms charge.
 
Saleshandy changed how I think about cold outreach. It's not about sending more emails. It's about sending the right emails to the right people at the right time.
 
Over to you: What's your biggest challenge with cold emails right now?

Most AI SDRs only send emails.
- They can’t call your leads.
- They can’t reply to objections.
- They can’t book meetings.

And don’t even get me started on traditional SDRs -
slow, expensive, inconsistent.

We built an Omnichannel AI SDR that does it all —

Calls leads, emails them, replies back, and
handles objection, for you.

Want it?
🟣 Comment "SDR" + Like the post.
🟣 Connect with me
🟣 I will send it over

The full pipeline:
→ Webhook captures every inbound form submission
→ Reoon verifies emails in real-time with fallback handling
→ Apify scrapes company websites, LinkedIn profiles & posts
→ AI reads every touchpoint — website copy, posts, team pages
→ 4 fully-personalized follow-ups go out over next 7 days.
→ Gmail API handles delivery & thread-aware replies
→ AI calls leads first — if no answer, a follow-up sequence auto-triggers

The technical backbone:
• Email validation + Slack alert if bounce risk.
• Website scraper to find more info.
• Apify scrapes LinkedIn, and their posts for deep research.
• Content crawler pulls recent wins, blog copy, tone of voice.
• Multi-agent AI analyzes all data to write original messages
• Branching flows adapt if lead replies, bounces, or no-shows
• Every email thread adapts based on context & response

What blew my mind:

The AI doesn’t just write one email and hope.

It reads the lead’s last LinkedIn post.

It knows their website tone, Instagram content, and product updates.
It references that in-line - mid-sentence —

Then... it calls them.
If they pick up — it books a meeting.

If not — a hyper-personalized 4 email sequence fires automatically.

But here’s where it gets wild:

If the lead replies,
→ The AI reads the thread
→ Handles objections
→ Sends natural, human-like follow-ups
→ And books the meeting for you — with zero manual effort

No templates. No manual intervention. No bottlenecks.

json.content:
A Job Search Routine That Works
One of the biggest mistakes I see?

People are job hunting in random bursts of panic.

Here’s a calm, sustainable routine that works:

Monday – Research & Shortlist 🗓 
Pick 15–20 high-quality roles. Prioritize new ones (<7 days old).

Tuesday – Resume Tailoring 🛠 
Use AI to tailor your resume for 3–5 top roles. Focus on the top third.

Wednesday – Outreach 📬
Send 3–5 short DMs to hiring managers. Be specific, not spammy.

Thursday – Apply & Track🚀 
Apply to your shortlist. Log everything (Bloom helps here, too).

Friday – Reflect & Iterate🔁 
Did you get replies? If not, try changing the subject line or your resume headline.

Consistency beats chaos.

And this system works.

Your smarter job search starts here. Join the waitlist → bloomhq.ai

#JobSearchTips #EntryLevelJobs #NewGradJobs #CareerAdvice #RemoteJobs #USA #NowHiring #Bloom

What if your customers didn’t browse your store, but their agent did? 🤖 

Forget endless scrolling, agentic commerce is about AI doing the shopping for you. It finds the right item, confirms it matches the brief and pays for you. 

In our latest blog post Checkout.com, explains what agentic commerce is, why it matters, and how to prepare for the future of shopping. 

Jorn Lambert, Chief Product Officer at Mastercard and Jack Forestell, Chief Product and Strategy Officer at Visa also shared their thoughts with us.

“The future of commerce will be powered by agentic AI, and it’s arriving faster than we could have ever imagined. Mastercard’s longstanding partnerships across the payments ecosystem, including our work with Checkout.com, are critical to delivering secure, seamless payments into the heart of AI-powered experiences,” said Jorn Lambert, Chief Product Officer at Mastercard. “We designed Mastercard Agent Pay to empower AI agents to transact safely and responsibly, grounded by the same trust and control consumers expect from Mastercard. Our work with Checkout.com is helping to directly enable businesses to meet customers where they are – inside the conversation.”

"We're big believers that AI agents will transform how people around the world shop and pay, and the first step is to ensure that payments are safe, secure and in the consumer’s control," said Jack Forestell, Chief Product and Strategy Officer at Visa. "With Visa Intelligent Commerce, we’re connecting the tech, payments and AI ecosystems to build the first-generation of trusted AI Commerce and we’re excited to partner with innovative companies like Checkout.com to bring this vision to life.”

Is your business ready for this next era? 👉  Find out

json.content:
Much of  Money20/20 Europe is inevitably about regulation, tech, infrastructure and some healthy guess work on AI (!)...so it was great to join a panel to discuss how marketing can help fintech brands differentiate in the long term.

We unpacked how fintech CMOs are staying close to business performance and gaining currency to build brands for the long term. 

Here were my key takeaways:
📈 It's obvious, but as a commercial function marketing should speak the lingua franca of the business.  Anything that is measured that is not is sheer vanity

🤝 Marketing has a responsibility to share all work with other commercial teams and move in sync. These partnerships build currency.   

📊 In a world of razor thin product differentiation, it's imperative to pick one thing your audience cares about and keep talking about it to build relative differentiation.  

🤖 AI has removed many of the irritable tasks for marketers, but we have to go back and redesign our work to liberate disruptive moves. 

Fintech operates within two driving forces that are in tension - regulation and innovation - what matters most is the creation of trust in order to simplify peoples world.

Session was covered well by Payment Expert https://lnkd.in/e5BnK35s

Thanks to Soha Hohnecker (she/her), Yewande Akomolafe-Kalu, and Kathryn Frankson, to Money20/20 Europe for the invitation, and to the audience for starting the day early!

#Money2020EU #WhereTheWorldChecksOut

During Mental Health Awareness Month in May, our teams around the world made time to pause, connect, and put wellbeing first 🌍

From mindfulness to muddy obstacle races, each activity reflected what matters most, supporting one another, #BeingTogether, and showing up as #OneTeam.

Here’s how teams across our offices championed mental health and created space for reflection and community:

🇦🇪 UAE

The team paused for a thoughtful session on recognizing internal triggers and learning practical strategies for managing stress, paired with a well-earned waffle break.

json.content:
The 2010s: Platforms, Power, and the Push for Control
Part 3 of 4 – The Road to Payment Orchestration

By the 2010s, payments had gone mainstream.

Stripe launched in 2011.
Adyen went public in 2018.
Square was already powering millions of small businesses.

Payments were no longer a problem to solve, they were a strategic growth lever.
APIs were clean, fast, and well-documented. Developers loved it.

But merchants? They started seeing the limits.

Suddenly, they weren’t just looking for a gateway or a processor.
They wanted:
🔹Global scale
🔹Smart routing
🔹Better approval rates
🔹Multiple PSPs — without multiple integrations

This era gave rise to... fragmentation.

Merchants stitched together their own stacks:
Stripe in the US, Adyen in Europe, local acquirers in Brazil, PayPal on the side.
It worked but it was fragile.

There was no single layer orchestrating it all.
No intelligent routing, no fallback logic, no unified reporting.

Payments were powerful, but messy.

This complexity is what gave rise to the next evolution:
Orchestration as a purpose-built layer.

Next: The 2020s — where orchestration became a must-have, not a nice-to-have.

json.content:
The 1990s: When Online Payments Were Born

Part 1 of 4 – The Road to Payment Orchestration

Payment orchestration is one of the hottest topics in payments today and for good reason.

It’s what allows modern businesses to route transactions through multiple PSPs, reduce failed payments, optimize for cost and performance, and stay agile across markets. Think of it like air traffic control for your payment stack, making sure every transaction gets where it needs to go, smoothly and efficiently.

But orchestration didn’t appear overnight.

It’s the result of decades of evolution in how we move money online.

So, over the next four posts, I’m tracing that evolution:
The 1990s, 2000s, 2010s, and 2020s.
Each era brought a major shift, and each one built the foundation for where we are now.

Let’s start at the very beginning.

The 1990s: When Online Payments Were Born

Amazon and eBay were launching.
The internet was still a curiosity to most.
And online payments? A wild experiment.

There were no payment platforms as we know them today.
No Stripe. No PayPal. No smart routing.

Just early gateways like CyberCash (1994) and Authorize.Net (1996),  primitive tools that allowed merchants to accept card payments online... if they were lucky.

These systems were crude:
🔹Manual reconciliation in spreadsheets
🔹Almost zero fraud controls
🔹Only basic US card support
🔹No redundancy — one gateway per merchant

And yet, it worked
just barely.

The big question back then wasn’t “How do we improve checkout?”
It was: Can we even make online payments happen at all?

The answer was yes. But it came at a cost.
For every $1 inconvenience, merchants paid $10 in operational pain.
Still, the foundation was laid.

In Part 2, we’ll move into the 2000s
where developers demanded more flexibility and the API-first movement reshaped what was possible. 

#Fintech #Payments #Ecommerce #Paymentorchestration #Startups #Onlinepayments #Historyofpayments #RoanDollmann

json.content:
AI in Fraud Detection: Smarter, Faster… but Still Ethical?

Every second, AI is scanning thousands of transactions.

Blink, and it’s already flagged a suspicious payment, blocked a fraudulent account, or saved a customer from a scam.

But with great power comes... well, the responsibility to not overreach.

Here’s how Amex and Revolut are navigating that line:
🔹 American Express uses real-time AI models to analyze spending patterns at the cardholder level. The system is so precise that it can differentiate between your grocery run and a fraudulent charge in milliseconds without locking you out for no reason.

🔹 Revolut takes it further with behavioral biometrics. If your typing rhythm or swipe pattern is off, their AI flags it. Combine that with location and device data, and their system can detect fraud before a transaction even happens.

The challenge?
AI can be too good, flagging legitimate activity, profiling users unfairly, or 
becoming a “black box” with no clear audit trail.

That’s why leading fintechs are now focused on ethical AI:
➡️ Transparent model governance
➡️ Bias testing and mitigation
➡️ Human review layers for edge cases

Fraud detection isn’t just about stopping bad actors
It’s about protecting users without treating them like criminals.

As AI gets faster and smarter, the real question is:
Can it also stay fair?

#FraudDetection #EthicalAI #FintechSecurity #ArtificialIntelligence #AML #ModelGovernance #BehavioralBiometrics #FintechTrends #Revolut #AmericanExpress #RoanDollmann

json.content:
Klarna vs. Visa
BNPL vs. Credit Cards
Same end result: debt.
Very different business models.

Visa charges the consumer interest.
Klarna charges the merchant a higher fee.

But here’s the twist:
BNPL feels free. Credit feels expensive.

And yet...
BNPL fees can hit 5–6% per transaction.
Credit card processing? Closer to 1.5–2.5%.

Merchants gladly pay more for BNPL. Why?
🔹Higher conversion rates
🔹Bigger baskets
🔹More impulse purchases
Because BNPL doesn’t feel like taking on debt — even though it is.

Klarna turns your checkout into a growth engine.
Visa turns your customer into a recurring revenue stream.

In Klarna's model, the merchant foots the bill.
In Visa's model, the consumer pays — if they carry a balance.

Klarna: Merchant pays → raises prices → everyone pays.
Visa: Consumer pays → interest + fees → if they don't pay in full.

BNPL isn’t a revolution.
It’s credit with a makeover.
A new interface on an old system — designed to feel different.

And it’s working, especially with younger consumers and those living paycheck-to-paycheck.
But defaults are rising.
Regulators are circling.
And the “interest-free” promise isn’t always what it seems.

So who’s really paying?
And how long can this model last?

In the end:
BNPL = short-term sales booster, long-term margin shrinker
Credit = long-term consumer liability, short-term convenience

Same outcome. Different incentives.
And no matter how you dress it up — debt is debt.

Are we building something better or just marketing it better?

#Klarna #Visa #BNPL #CreditCards #FintechInsights #PaymentProcessing #CheckoutConversion #ConsumerCredit #DebtEconomy #RetailStrategy #EcommerceGrowth #RoanDollmann

Part 5 is here and it’s all about failure.

Specifically, how to turn failed payments into recovered revenue with primary & retry routing.

Dynamic Payment Routing – Part 5: Primary & Retry Logic

Every failed payment is a lost customer.

And most of those failures? They’re avoidable.

Routing Logic #5: Primary & Retry Routing

Even the best PSPs have bad days.
Downtime, network errors, or false fraud flags can cause a perfectly valid transaction to fail.

Smart orchestration doesn’t stop there.
It reroutes the transaction to a backup PSP in real-time.

🔹 If PSP 1 fails → Retry via PSP 2
🔹 Instant failover, no friction
🔹 Zero code changes from the customer’s side

Real-world impact:
A subscription-based merchant using a single PSP had a 9% failure rate on recurring payments.

After implementing retry logic with a secondary PSP, they automatically recovered nearly 60% of the failed transactions.

That’s pure recovered revenue.

Payments fail.
The question is: do you have a Plan B or just a dead end?

Stay tuned for part 6: Performance-Based Routing

What is Open Banking and why is everyone from Stripe to Tink betting on it?

For decades, banks operated in silos.
Your financial data was locked away.
No sharing. No flexibility. No innovation.

Open Banking changed that.

At its core, Open Banking is the secure, regulated sharing of financial data between banks and third-party providers with your consent.

It powers:
🔹 Real-time account verification
🔹 Smarter credit decisions
🔹 Unified finance dashboards
🔹 Instant bank payments (no cards needed)

Instead of relying on slow ACH transfers or expensive card rails, Open Banking connects directly to your bank account in real-time.

And it's no longer just a European experiment.

Meet the key players:

➡️Tink (acquired by Visa) — powering financial data access across Europe
➡️ Plaid — connects 12,000+ U.S. institutions
➡️ TrueLayer — fuels Open Banking for Revolut & Wise
➡️ Stripe — launched Financial Connections to tap into the API economy
➡️ Banked — helps merchants accept direct bank payments
➡️ Yapily — builds deep integrations across UK and EU banks

The result?
A new generation of fintech products, lenders, and platforms that bypass traditional infrastructure altogether.

Faster. Cheaper. More flexible.

6AMLD: The Anti-Money Laundering Rule That Could Impact Your Fintech - Even If You're Not in the EU

When Revolut froze thousands of accounts...
When Binance got fined over €4 billion...
When N26 and Wise tightened onboarding...

It wasn’t just about KYC. It was about liability.
Enter the 6th Anti-Money Laundering Directive (6AMLD)
Europe’s quiet revolution in compliance law.

Here’s what it means:
➡️ “Enabling” financial crime is now a crime.
If your platform facilitates money laundering even unknowingly, you’re accountable.
➡️ Corporate liability is official.
It’s not just compliance officers anymore. Under 6AMLD, companies can be criminally charged.
➡️ Those fuzzy gray areas? They’re now black and white.
➡️ Minimum 4-year sentence. EU-wide.
 No more country-by-country leniency. Harmonization = high stakes.

Why fintech should care:
 🔹 BaaS platforms are now risk carriers
 🔹 PSPs onboarding sub-merchants at scale
 🔹 Cross-border apps managing high volumes 

If you're part of the value chain - you're part of the compliance chain.

This isn’t just EU regulation.
This is a global signal: Regulators expect prevention, not just reporting.
Startups can no longer afford to treat AML as a checkbox.

I'm curious to hear how others are adapting: what’s your approach to liability in the age of embedded finance?

Dynamic Payment Routing – Part 8: Business Case–Based Routing

Not all payments are created equal and today, we’re proving it.
In Part 8, we explore how routing logic should change depending on whether you're handling a consumer checkout or a B2B invoice.

Routing Logic #8: Business Case–Based Routing

The context of a transaction matters.

🔹 A €50 consumer checkout is not the same as a €50,000 business invoice.
🔹 B2B payments may involve wire transfers, payment terms, or invoicing flows.
🔹 B2C transactions often require instant authorization, fraud checks, and higher conversion UX.

Yet many merchants route both the same way, through a generic payment flow.

Smart orchestration adjusts routing logic based on the business use case:
➡️Consumer payments → route to PSPs optimized for card payments, fraud prevention, and UX.
➡️Business payments → route to PSPs with better support for bank transfers, higher-value transactions, and custom reconciliation.

Example:
A SaaS platform serving both individual users and enterprise clients initially used one PSP for everything.

After splitting B2C flows to Stripe and B2B flows to a PSP optimized for invoicing and high-value wire transfers, they:

➡️Reduced processing friction for business clients
➡️Improved approval rates for consumer cards
➡️Enabled flexible billing without reworking their entire backend

Your routing logic should know your customer and their intent.

One more to go!
Stay tuned for part 9: Risk Profile Routing

Let’s keep it moving -Part 6 of the series is here.

And we’re exploring one of the smartest routing upgrades out there: using real-time performance data to guide every transaction.

Dynamic Payment Routing – Part 6: Performance-Based Routing

What if your PSP is underperforming and you don’t even know it?

Routing Logic #6: Performance-Based Routing

Most payment setups are static. Your route to PSP A, always.
But PSP A’s success rate isn’t always the same. It changes by:
🔹 Time of day
🔹 Geography
🔹 Transaction type
🔹 PSP load or downtime

Smart orchestration monitors real-time performance and routes to the best-performing PSP at that moment.

➡️PSP A has 91% approvals
➡️PSP B has 96% approvals
🔁Route to PSP B automatically

Real-world example:
An eCommerce brand operating across Europe saw fluctuating approval rates, especially on mobile traffic at night.

After enabling real-time performance-based routing, they recovered 5–7% more transactions, with no changes to their checkout.

Set it once. Let the logic adapt.

If your routing hasn’t changed in months, it’s not optimized, it’s outdated.

Stay tuned for part 7: Geolocation Routing

json.content:
Ever wonder what actually happens when you click "Pay Now"?

Online payments may feel instant, but behind the scenes, there’s a complex web of handshakes, approvals, and verifications.

Here’s what happens—step by step:

1. Checkout
You enter your card or choose a payment method (like PayPal, Klarna, or Apple Pay).
→ This data is captured and encrypted.
 
2. Payment Gateway
Think Stripe, Adyen, or PayFirmly.
→ The gateway securely routes the transaction data to the payment processor.
 
3. Payment Processor
The processor (like FIS, Worldpay, or Checkout.com) contacts:
🔹Card networks (Visa, Mastercard, etc.)
🔹Your issuing bank (the bank that gave you the card)
 
4. Authorization
Your bank checks:
 🔹Is the card real?
 🔹Is there enough balance?
 🔹Is the transaction suspicious?
If approved → a temporary hold is placed. If declined → you’re shown an error.
 
5. Response Sent Back
The approval or decline flows back through the processor → gateway → merchant website.
→ You see “Payment Successful.”
 
6. Settlement
At the end of the day, funds are moved between banks via batch clearing.
→ This is when the money actually starts moving.

Bonus: Orchestration
Platforms like PayFirmly offer smart routing, fallback logic, and local optimization across 500+ methods, so your payment doesn’t fail just because one processor is down.

In short:
What looks like one click is actually a high-speed relay race between 5+ players.
 
Next time your payment fails… it’s probably not your fault.

#Fintech #Payments #eCommerce #DigitalPayments #PaymentProcessing #PayFirmly #HowItWorks #Stripe #Adyen #Visa #PayPal #Klarna #Mastercard #SmartRouting

json.content:
Welcome back to the series!

In Part 1, we explored how routing based on card type can cut costs and boost approvals.

Today, we’re diving into another silent killer: FX fees.

Dynamic Payment Routing – Part 2: Currency-Based Logic

You're selling internationally. Your customers are paying in GBP, EUR, USD, AED...

But here’s the hidden cost most merchants overlook:

FX markups and bad exchange rates can silently eat into 1–2% of your revenue.

Routing Logic #2: Currency-Based Routing

When a customer pays in a local currency, smart payment orchestration doesn't just check if the PSP supports it; it asks:

Which PSP offers the best exchange rate and lowest FX fees right now?
🔹GBP → PSP 1
🔹EUR → PSP 2
🔹USD → PSP 3

Route each transaction based on currency → fee structure → real value.

A merchant I spoke to found that their main PSP was charging 2.5% on GBP payouts.
Switching to a better-suited PSP for that currency saved over €20,000 annually with no change in user flow.

FX fees aren’t just a finance problem anymore.
They’re a routing opportunity.

Stay tuned for Part 3: Transaction Amount-Based Routing!

json.content:
Dynamic Payment Routing – Part 9: Risk Profile Routing

Let’s wrap the series with one of the most powerful and underrated tactics:

Routing based on risk.

Because not every customer poses the same level of fraud, chargeback, or compliance risk. So why route them all the same?

Routing Logic #9: Risk Profile–Based Routing

Smart orchestration doesn’t just route based on card type or region.
It asks: What’s the risk score of this transaction?

And then routes accordingly:
🔹 Low-risk customer → fast, low-cost PSP
🔹 Medium-risk → route to PSP with stronger auth and fraud controls
🔹 High-risk → use fallback PSP with risk mitigation, 3DS, or manual review

Example:

A digital platform serving both EU and LATAM users used risk scoring to identify patterns:
➡️Customers with long histories and matched billing info = low-risk
➡️First-time users in high-fraud geos = high risk

They routed low-risk flows to a high-approval PSP and high-risk flows through a gateway with enhanced fraud detection + 3DS.

Result?
➡️ Lower fraud losses
➡️ Higher net approvals
➡️ Less friction for good users

Risk-aware routing turns your PSP stack into a fraud management tool, not just a payment pipe.

Thanks for following along through all 9 parts of the Dynamic Payment Routing series.

#RussianLanguageDay

Сегодня, 6 июня, мы отмечаем Международный день русского языка — праздник, учреждённый ООН в 2010 г. в честь дня рождения великого русского поэта Александра Сергеевича Пушкина.

Пушкин — это не только основоположник современного русского литературного языка, но и культурный мост между эпохами и народами. 

Его язык — живая ткань русской души, а его стихи цитируют даже те, кто не говорит по-русски.

🖋️ "Я памятник себе воздвиг нерукотворный..." — эта строка известна далеко за пределами России, как символ силы слова и вечной ценности культуры.

В мире, полном перемен, язык остаётся опорой идентичности и важнейшим инструментом диалога.

📚 Сегодня — отличный повод вспомнить, что язык формирует мышление, объединяет людей и помогает строить мосты, а не стены.

С праздником всех, кто говорит, пишет, чувствует и думает на русском!

И любит русскую культуру.

#Russia #Pushkin #RussianLanguageDay

json.content:
Back with Part 4 of the Dynamic Payment Routing series!

Today we’re diving into a powerful but underused tactic: on-us routing using BIN data.

Dynamic Payment Routing – Part 4: On-Us Routing (Using BIN)

Ever paid someone using the same bank, and the money landed instantly?

That’s called an on-us transaction when the issuing bank and acquiring bank are the same.

And yes, it works in card payments too.

Routing Logic #4: On-Us Routing (Using BIN)

Every card number carries a BIN (Bank Identification Number), the first few digits that identify the issuing bank.

Smart routing checks the BIN in real time and asks:
➡️Is the card from Bank A?
➡️Can I route this through the same bank (or acquirer) for faster, cheaper settlement?

On-us transactions often come with:
🔹Lower interchange fees
🔹Faster clearing
🔹Higher approval rates
🔹Reduced fraud risk

Real-world example:
A European merchant saw a 5–10% uplift in approval rates when routing transactions through a PSP that could process on-us with a major local bank.

Their legacy PSP didn’t even check for it.

Some PSPs actively support BIN-based logic to optimize routing, including Stripe and Checkout.com.

Stripe uses BIN data via Radar and its global acquiring stack to localize processing and improve approval rates.

Checkout.com leverages BINs to detect issuer behavior and enable on-us routing when both card and merchant run through its acquiring rails.

In payments, proximity matters. 
And nothing’s closer than on-us.

Stay tuned for part 5: Primary & Retry Routing

#Payments #Fintech #SmartRouting #BINRouting #CardProcessing #MerchantOptimization #OnUs #PaymentOrchestration #RevenueStrategy #HighRiskPayments #B2B #B2

#RussiaToday

Dear friends, as you can imagine, I couldn't miss today's Forum of the Future 2050 in #Moscow, where the headliner was the rock star of world diplomacy, #SergeyLavrov :)

At the panel discussion "#MultipolarWorld in the 21st Century" Lavrov "lit it up").

"Now, too, we are talking about the fight between good and evil." The Russian minister noted that the #West and #Europe are on the side of evil.

"And not all of Europe, by the way. But this aggressive core, led by Starmers, Mertzes, Macrons," Lavrov emphasized.

"They are fighting against us, supplying Ukraine with high-precision, high-tech weapons that Ukrainians cannot control, which means that the citizens of those countries that supply these weapons control them."

"In addition to this, they also want to demonstrate the isolation of our country by prohibiting everyone from coming here," Lavrov noted.

Participants of the discussion:

Moderator – #DimitrySimes, American-Russian historian and publisher of the American magazine The National Interest

#ErrolMusk, engineer, businessman, gem trader, father of #ElonMusk

#GeorgeGalloway, master of British politics

#LarryJohnson, former CIA and US State Department analyst

#KarinKneissl, former Foreign Minister of Austria

#ZhangWeiwei, is a Chinese professor of international relations at Fudan University and the director of its China Institute.

Lavrov: "Multipolarity is a geopolitical reality."

On the #UN: "It would be best to move the UN to #Sochi" - thunderous applause and laughter in the audience 😊

On #Merz and #Germany: when Merz says that Russia will not stop at Ukraine, he judges by himself. This is genetic revanchism. We are not fighting for territories, but for the Russian people.

About the UN: "What did the UN do to anyone?" Everything is written correctly there, it is only necessary to implement it - and implement it not selectively, as the West prefers, but systematically.

Sergey Lavrov recalled the mass murder of Russians in #Odessa.

"To the #shame of the entire #West - uninvestigated crimes, including such significant ones as the burning of fifty people alive in the House of Trade Unions in Odessa on May 2, 2014."

And also about other crimes, which the collective West chose not to pay attention to and spoke out about the hypocrisy of the so-called collective West, which itself provoked the tragedy in #Serbia, tearing away Kosovo, and then ... pretended that the events in Ukraine in 2014 do not imply the right of people to self-determination.

More precisely, only those who are under the patronage of Western curators can self-determination.

Well, I will finish the story with another symptomatic quote from Sergey Lavrov: “Right now, Russia’s best diplomats are fighters on the front lines.”

P.S. Thank you, #KonstantinMalofeev (Head of #Tsargrad), for organizing this cool event!

#Russia #FairPlay #Dialogue

#LavrovToday
Europe once again rallying under Nazi flag against Russia – #Lavrov

The top diplomat has hit out at Ukraine’s #EU backers over their renewed push to inflict a strategic defeat on #Moscow.

The EU has failed to learn the lessons of history by once again gathering under a Nazi flag to attempt to inflict a strategic defeat on Russia, Foreign Minister Sergey Lavrov said on Tuesday.

Lavrov was responding to remarks by German Chancellor #FriedrichMerz, who on Monday declared there were “no more range limitations” for weapons sent to Ukraine by the West, including Germany. 

#Merz has also vowed to turn the German military into Europe’s strongest conventional force.

“#Europe has once again found itself a Nazi flag by committing to a completely misguided, disastrous venture of inflicting a strategic defeat on Russia,”- #SergeyLavrov stated.

“To hear from the current German leader that #Germany will regain its position as the leading military power in Europe, just after we have celebrated the 80th anniversary of the defeat of Hitler’s Nazism, is quite symptomatic. History, apparently, taught these people nothing.”

Merz’s remarks on long-range weapons drew concern even within his own coalition. German Vice Chancellor and Finance Minister #LarsKlingbeil told reporters shortly after the chancellor’s statement that no agreements had been reached regarding the range of Ukrainian strikes using German weapons on Russian territory beyond what the previous government had in place. 

Lavrov said Merz’s confused and uninformed position “reflects the level of competence of modern European leaders.”

While Germany has not supplied long-range weapons to #Ukraine, Merz’s comments have stirred speculation about a possible policy shift, particularly regarding the delivery of Taurus cruise missiles. Former Chancellor Olaf Scholz consistently rejected the idea, warning it would dangerously escalate the conflict.

Lavrov also addressed remarks by French Foreign Minister #JeanNoelBarrot, who on Monday claimed #France is not at war with Russia.

“He’s not telling the truth. France is fighting against Russia because its long-range missiles are being used by the Nazi regime in Kiev to attack the territory of the Russian Federation. And these are mainly attacks on civilian targets,” Lavrov said.

He stressed that the only path to ending the Ukraine conflict lies in halting Europe’s militaristic course.

“The main thing here... is to ensure that Europe stops sabotaging the movement towards peace, which is supported by both the #UnitedStates and #Türkiye, and to which the #RussianFederation is fully committed,” Lavrov said.
 
#RussiaToday #Russia #Putin #FairPlay #CommonSense

Dynamic Payment Routing – Part 7: Geolocation Routing

We’ve talked about routing by card type, currency, and transaction amount but now it’s time to zoom out and look at where your customer is coming from.

Because if you're not routing based on location, you're likely leaving approvals (and money) on the table.

Routing Logic #7: Geolocation-Based Routing

A German customer.
A Spanish PSP.
A cross-border fee, higher fraud flagging, and lower approval rate.

Why?

Because the transaction wasn’t routed locally.

Smart orchestration uses the customer’s location (billing address, IP, BIN data) to:

🔹 Route to a local PSP
🔹 Reduce cross-border fees
🔹 Improve trust with local issuers
🔹 Boost approval rates

Example:
A merchant selling digital subscriptions in Germany was routing all EU traffic through a PSP based in Ireland.

By switching German traffic to a local acquiring partner, they cut processing fees by 1.3% and saw a 6% increase in successful payments.

In payments, geography isn’t just for shipping, it’s for routing too.

#Payments #Fintech #SmartRouting #Geolocation #CrossBorderPayments #PSPStrategy #HighRiskPayments #MerchantOptimization #PaymentOrchestration #RevenueGrowth #B2B #B2C

json.content:
It’s that time again!
Welcome back to the Dynamic Payment Routing series.

Today we’re diving into how transaction size can impact your payment costs more than you think.

Dynamic Payment Routing – Part 3: Transaction Amount Logic

Would you process a €5 coffee the same way you process a €5,000 laptop?

Most merchants do. And that’s a problem.

Routing Logic #3: Transaction Amount-Based Routing

Not all payment volumes should take the same path.

Here’s why:
🔹 Low-value transactions are hit hardest by fixed per-transaction fees.
🔹 High-value transactions suffer more from percentage-based fees and approval risk.

Smart routing evaluates the transaction size and dynamically selects the PSP that’s most cost-effective for that amount.

Example setup:
➡️€1–20 → PSP 1 (low fixed fees, ideal for microtransactions)
➡️€20–500 → PSP 2 (balanced fee structure)
➡️€500+ → PSP 3 (best approval rates for high-value payments)

A merchant we worked with found that their default PSP charged €0.30 + 2.9%. 

That meant:
➡️A €5 payment costs them 9.7% in fees
➡️A €2,000 payment risked a failed approval on a single gateway

By routing based on transaction size, they cut payment fees by 18% overall.

Same product. Same customer. Different transaction logic.
That’s orchestration.

Stay tuned for part 4: On-Us Routing (Using BIN)

#Payments #Fintech #SmartRouting #TransactionFees #PaymentStrategy #HighRiskPayments #MerchantOptimization #RevenueStrategy #B2B #PaymentOrchestration

json.content:
What is Payment Tokenization?

Payment tokenization is the process of replacing sensitive payment data (like your credit card number) with a unique, non-sensitive placeholder, which is a token.

The original data is stored securely in a vault.
The token is what gets used in the transaction, and it’s worthless if intercepted.

Example:
When you add your card to Apple Pay, your actual card number is never stored on the device or shared with the merchant.

Instead, it’s replaced with a token, which is a randomly generated code that acts as a stand-in.

That token is useless on its own.
If stolen, it’s just a string of numbers with no connection to your card.
The real card data? Safely stored in a secure vault.

Why does this matter?

Tokenization helps:
🔹Prevent card data theft
🔹Reduce PCI compliance burden
🔹Enable one-click checkouts and recurring billing
🔹️Power mobile wallets and invisible payments
🔹Safely route transactions across multiple providers

Without tokenization, every transaction is a security risk.
With it, you get speed, safety, and scale.

It’s not a luxury.
It’s the foundation.

#PaymentTokenization #SecurePayments #DigitalPayments #Tokenization #CardSecurity #PCICompliance #InvisiblePayments #PaymentProcessing #FintechExplained #OneClickCheckout #MobileWallets #PaymentInfrastructure #FintechEducation #DataSecurity #RoanDollmann

#VladimirPutin
"We need a fundamentally new development model, built not on the rules of #neocolonialism, when the so-called golden billion pumps out the resources of other countries in the interests of a narrow circle of so-called elites," Putin said.

Global challenges certainly require a global response. It is impossible to solve problems alone or at someone else's expense. Only joint actions can ensure the movement of the entire #civilization forward.

The #BRICS countries set the bar in the development of nuclear energy, the IT industry, and the AI ​​sphere. The principles of the organization are parity and openness to new participants. Russia invites partners to contribute to new global growth.

The global economy is undergoing the largest transformation in recent decades.

If at the beginning of the 21st century the BRICS countries made up a fifth of the world economy, now this figure has grown to 47 percent. It is obvious that this figure will grow.

"This is, as they say, a medical fact. This will happen inevitably."

"As for the payment infrastructure, here we will continue to closely cooperate with our BRICS colleagues, build effective, reliable and, I repeat, independent of external interference mechanisms and services," Putin said.


About the state of the #RussianEconomy

• "The Russian economy must become more technological. And this is not a wish, this is an objective requirement of today and tomorrow. A challenge that must be met if we want to become stronger. And we do."

• According to Putin, the idea of ​​the Russian economy as a raw materials economy is "outdated." "The contribution of the raw materials component to the economic dynamics of our country is no longer decisive. Our economy is not just developing confidently, but is becoming more qualitative, complex and multifaceted."

• "Despite the difficult external environment, Russia's GDP has increased by more than 4% annually over the past two years, i.e. it has grown at a rate higher than the #globalaverage," the president said. In particular, non-oil and gas GDP growth in 2023 was 7.2%, and in 2024 — 4.9% (RBC)

• Russia has achieved a record reduction in poverty: at the end of last year, it was 7.2%. In 2000, the poverty rate in Russia was 29%.

• Unemployment is 2.3%, a historical minimum.

• Youth unemployment in Russia is 7.5%, one of the best rates in the #world.

• "Overall, the level of wages in Russia is still, of course, insufficient." Russia needs to move to an economy of high wages based on increased labor productivity.

• The most important task for 2025 is the transition of the Russian economy to balanced growth.

json.content:
The 2000s: When Payments Went Programmable
Part 2 of 4 – The Road to Payment Orchestration

The 1990s proved that online payments could work.
The 2000s asked: how can we make them better?

Developers were done settling for clunky, rigid gateways.
They wanted control, flexibility, and most of all, simplicity.

Enter the API-first movement.

For the first time, developers could integrate payments directly into their apps with code. No faxing forms. No 6-week onboarding. No cryptic legacy software.

The shift was massive.

In the 2000s, we saw:
 🔹 The rise of PayPal, making online payments consumer-friendly
 🔹 The launch of Braintree (2007), with cleaner APIs and tokenization
 🔹 The early days of mobile and app commerce
 🔹 A growing global appetite for digital transactions

And critically
we moved from gateways to platforms.

Platforms that abstracted the mess of acquiring banks, card schemes, and compliance headaches while giving developers more power than ever before.

But there was still a catch...

Merchants had to choose one provider and live with the trade-offs:

Want better global coverage? Too bad, your PSP isn’t strong in LATAM.
Payment failures rising? You’re stuck until they fix it.
Negotiating fees? No leverage when you’re locked in.

The orchestration era hadn’t arrived yet.
But the groundwork was being laid.

Up next: The 2010s — where Stripe, Adyen, and others took payments mainstream and merchants began demanding real control.

#fintech #payments #paymentorchestration #startups #onlinepayments #developerstack #APIfirst #RoanDollmann

Kyiv's sponsors are accomplices of terrorists - Putin

The bombing of trains in the Bryansk and Kursk regions is "certainly a terrorist act", - Putin

"The #attack on the #civilian population was carried out intentionally. This only confirms our fears that the already illegitimate regime in Kyiv, which once seized power, is gradually degenerating into a terrorist organization. And its sponsors are becoming accomplices of terrorists," the head of state said at a meeting with the government.

"What happened in Bryansk, in the Bryansk region, is a targeted attack on #civilians. According to all international norms, such actions are called #terrorism."

The Kiev leadership, against the backdrop of huge losses at the front, trying to intimidate Russia, has moved on to organizing terrorist attacks.

The Ukrainian Armed Forces are retreating along the entire line of combat, suffering one defeat after another.

"The current Kyiv regime does not need peace at all.
Peace for it, most likely, means the loss of power."

The Kyiv authorities do not have political culture, allowing themselves to insult those with whom they are trying to negotiate:

"What kind of authority can the leaders of a thoroughly rotten and completely corrupt regime have? The whole world is talking about this."


#Putin and #Trump

#DonaldTrump: I just finished a telephone conversation with Russian President #VladimirPutin. The call lasted about an hour and 15 minutes.

We discussed the Ukrainian attack on aircraft parked in Russia, as well as other attacks on both sides.

It was a good conversation, but not one that will lead to immediate peace.

President Putin did say, and very strongly, that he will have to respond to the recent attack on the airfields."

A telephone conversation took place between the President of Russia and Pope Leo XIV.

During the exchange of opinions on the situation in Ukraine, Vladimir Putin confirmed his interest in achieving peace through political and diplomatic means, and stressed that for a final, fair and comprehensive resolution of the crisis, its root causes must be eliminated.

Putin informed about the specific agreements reached during the second round on the exchange of prisoners of war and the bodies of the dead.

▪️The Russian leader expressed hope that the Holy See will be more active in supporting religious freedom in Ukraine.

▪️The Pope spoke in favor of continuing the important dialogue between the two sister churches.

🚨 Coinbase launches Stablecoin Payments service for E-Commerce.

Here is what you should know:

Coinbase is launching a platform designed to make stablecoins a go-to payment method for online transactions, a potentially big leap forward in the mainstream adoption of cryptocurrencies meant to track the US dollar. 

The announcement follows the passage of landmark stablecoin legislation in the US Senate on Tuesday (see video below👇) and comes as large retailers have expressed interest in exploring stablecoins for use as payments in online transactions, a roughly $𝟲 𝘁𝗿𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝗶𝗻𝗱𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗿𝘆. 

Such use of this type of cryptocurrency threatens to circumvent card networks Visa Inc. and Mastercard Inc. and other online payment services.

“We built the new system to mimic credit-card rails so it slots into existing flows with zero disruption,” a Coinbase spokesperson said in a statement: https://lnkd.in/ddb2ZuDz

Coinbase Payments is targeting online platforms like Shopify Inc.and eBay Inc., a prized client segment among payment processors since the websites provide distribution to thousands of small-to-medium sized businesses who are often looking for ways to avoid the fees associated with accepting card payments.

The Coinbase payments products include a checkout suite to help consumers easily pay from a crypto wallet provided by Coinbase Wallet, MetaMask or Phantom, among others. 

Stripe recently announced plans to acquire Privy, which helps merchants embed crypto wallets into their websites:  https://lnkd.in/dYUdbisA

That allows them to accept stablecoin payments without requiring customers to exit to a third-party site to set up a wallet, which adds friction to the buying experience and lowers the chance of a customer completing their purchase.

Source video: CNBC

json.content:
Nubank is now the 𝗽𝗿𝗶𝗺𝗮𝗿𝘆 𝗯𝗮𝗻𝗸 for 𝟮𝟭.𝟳% of Brazilians 🤯

Probably nothing... 

A recent study by consultancy Okiar reveals that Nubank has become the main bank for 21.7% of Brazilians 🇧🇷 in 2025, surpassing traditional institutions like Itaú and Bradesco.

In the first half of 2025, Okiar surveyed over 1,000 Brazilians to analyze their relationships with financial institutions. 

The research showed that, on average, each person holds four bank accounts, highlighting the wide range of options in Brazil’s banking sector.

According to Okiar, the top six primary banks in Brazil are:

1️⃣ Nubank – 21.7%
2️⃣ Itaú – 14.2%
3️⃣ Bradesco – 12%
4️⃣ Caixa Econômica – 10%
5️⃣ Banco do Brasil – 9.9%
6️⃣ Santander – 8.1%

While Nubank ranks first as the most frequently cited “main bank,” it sits in third place when ranked by total number of customers. 

Caixa Econômica leads in overall customer base, followed by Bradesco, with Itaú and Banco do Brasil rounding out the top five.

This gap highlights a shift in consumer behavior, while traditional banks still hold the largest customer bases, digital banks like Nubank are increasingly becoming users’ primary financial institutions.

The study also found that customers of traditional banks use an average of ten different financial products, compared to eight for digital bank customers. 

This suggests that, despite the rise of digital banking, traditional banks continue to offer a broader range of services and maintain a strong presence in specific market segments.

Source/more info: https://lnkd.in/d9NpZSZi

json.content:
🚨 𝙅𝙐𝙎𝙏 𝙄𝙉: Coinbase secures EU 🇪🇺 Crypto License, and swaps Ireland for Luxembourg as main hub.

Coinbase has secured a license from Luxembourg to offer crypto services across the European Union and will make the country its central hub in the region.

The U.S. crypto exchange’s main European base has been in Ireland since 2023.

Coinbase said Friday that it obtained its Markets in Crypto Assets (MiCA) license from Luxembourg’s Commission de Surveillance du Secteur Financier (CSSF).

MiCA is a sweeping regulation that aims to create a harmonized legal framework for crypto across all 27 EU member states. 

The rules, which came fully into force late last year, also aim to reduce risks for consumers buying crypto assets following a series of scandals in the sector.

It makes Coinbase the first U.S. crypto exchange to receive a MiCA license. 

Rival firm Gemini which is owned by the Winklevoss twins, is expected to receive its own EU license from Malta soon. Gemini chose Malta as its MiCA hub in January.

“Coinbase is all in on Europe, and we’re advocating for crypto’s future across the continent,” Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong told CNBC: https://lnkd.in/dJgeuAKb

json.content:
🚨 𝙅𝙐𝙎𝙏 𝙄𝙉: X to offer investment and trading in ‘super app’ push. Foray into financial services could also include introducing a credit or debit card, CEO Linda Yaccarino says in an interview:

“𝙔𝙤𝙪’𝙡𝙡 𝙗𝙚 𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚 𝙩𝙤 𝙘𝙤𝙢𝙚 𝙩𝙤 𝙓 𝙖𝙣𝙙 𝙗𝙚 𝙖𝙗𝙡𝙚 𝙩𝙤 𝙩𝙧𝙖𝙣𝙨𝙖𝙘𝙩 𝙮𝙤𝙪𝙧 𝙬𝙝𝙤𝙡𝙚 𝙛𝙞𝙣𝙖𝙣𝙘𝙞𝙖𝙡 𝙡𝙞𝙛𝙚 𝙤𝙣 𝙩𝙝𝙚 𝙥𝙡𝙖𝙩𝙛𝙤𝙧𝙢,” Yaccarino said in an interview with the Financial Times at the Cannes Lions advertising festival:  https://lnkd.in/dhexPeYi

“And that’s whether I can pay you for the pizza that we shared last night or make an investment or a trade. So that’s the future.” 

She added that the company was also exploring the introduction of an X credit or debit card, which could come as soon as this year. 

The proposed foray into financial services comes as Musk seeks to model the platform, which he bought in 2022, after China’s WeChat, a one-stop shop for messaging, payments and shopping. 

X has already said it will be introducing 𝗫 𝗠𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘆, a digital wallet and peer-to-peer payment service, with Visa as its first partner later this year: https://lnkd.in/d5Wr7A8u

Yaccarino on Tuesday added that 𝗫 𝗠𝗼𝗻𝗲𝘆 would launch in the US 🇺🇸 first before being rolled out elsewhere, and said that the service would allow users to buy merchandise, store value or tip creators on the platform.

Source video: Axios

🚨 𝙅𝙐𝙎𝙏 𝙄𝙉: X app code points to a physical card coming to X Money.

Here is what we know:

X’s plans for a payments service may extend beyond the digital realm, new data suggests. 

According to findings from mobile app intelligence firm AppSensa, the X app has been updated over the past few weeks with several references related to a physical debit card, which can be customized with your X username. 

Dozens of new strings of code in the X app reference various actions you can take with the new debit card, including checking its shipping status, activating your card after it arrives, reporting your card lost or stolen, locking the card, setting a PIN, and more. 

Multiple strings also include the terms “physical_card_option” as well as just “physical,” the data indicates.

The news follows X CEO Linda Yaccarino’s January announcement that Visa would be the company’s first partner for the X Money service, which will allow users to instantly fund their X Wallet accounts via Visa Direct: https://lnkd.in/d5Wr7A8u

Plus, the code suggests X could be pursuing relationships with other partners, as both MasterCard and Amex are listed as supported payment card brands (Ssee printscreens below👇).

To support its venture into payments, X has been registering for money transmitter licenses across the U.S. for over a year. 

As of December 2023, the company was licensed for payment processing in a dozen states. It has since increased its registrations to include 40 states, plus Washington, D.C. : https://lnkd.in/eu6BfA4k

Source/more info: https://lnkd.in/ej9Gmwcv

🚨 𝘽𝙍𝙀𝘼𝙆𝙄𝙉𝙂: Revolut is quietly creating its own stablecoin.

Here is what we know:

Revolut is continuing its pursuit of creating its own stablecoin, sources familiar with the matter told Decrypt:  https://lnkd.in/d85dP_HB

The development comes amid a growing list of non-crypto companies now considering issuing their own stablecoins as the regulatory environment shifts in the United States and around the world.

The London-based bank launched Revolut X, a centralized crypto exchange available across the European Union, in 2024: https://lnkd.in/dhwS3825

The company is now eyeing its own stablecoin to further its crypto venture, two sources familiar with the company's plans told Decrypt. 

Rumors of a Revolut stablecoin have been circulating for months (https://lnkd.in/dh5mT9Tz), though the neobank is now in conversation with at least one crypto-native company regarding the venture, one of the sources said.

json.content:
🚨 𝙅𝙐𝙎𝙏 𝙄𝙉: Revolut crackdown coming in Hungary? 

New Tax could tighten rules for neobanks:

In recent years, Revolut has become a serious competitor to traditional Hungarian 🇭🇺 banks. 

While its presence in the savings market remains relatively small, over 𝟮 𝗺𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗶𝗼𝗻 Hungarian users now rely on the platform for purchases, currency exchange, and money transfers. 

This growing popularity is putting increasing pressure on traditional banks, especially since Revolut and other neobanks have often been exempt from regulatory burdens that local financial institutions must bear.

However, this may soon change.

A new spring tax package proposed by the Hungarian government specifically targets providers like Revolut. 

The legislation would tighten the rules around transaction fees, effectively closing the loophole that neobanks have used until now. 

According to the new definition, any transaction that reduces a user’s account balance could be subject to the fee, even if it’s just an internal transfer within the app, reports Telex: https://lnkd.in/dq8vRdzd

Revolut maintains that it complies fully with transaction tax obligations, but traditional banks argue that while they must pay fees even on transfers between accounts within their own bank, Revolut likely avoids paying fees for transfers within its own ecosystem.

The new (but not yet active) legal text now states that any payment service that reduces the user’s account balance should incur the tax, regardless of whether the movement is internally settled or not. 

This includes so-called internal “ledger movements,” where a balance decreases in one place and increases elsewhere within the same system—something neobanks previously argued didn’t constitute a real “transaction.”

The law has already been passed by Parliament and is now awaiting the president’s signature. 

Once enacted, it won’t just affect Revolut, other neobanks like the Hungarian startup BinX will also need to comply.

Traditional banks have long criticized the current imbalance. 

While they are subject to strict domestic regulations, including a bank tax, windfall tax, interest rate caps, and ATM installation mandates, foreign-backed FinTechs have often been spared these obligations.

The aim of the new law is to level the playing field. 

Time will tell whether this legislation truly closes the loophole or simply marks another chapter in the ongoing regulatory tug-of-war between traditional banks and rising neobanks.

json.content:
𝗦𝘁𝗼𝗿𝘆 𝗼𝗳 𝘀𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝗲𝘃𝗼𝗹𝘂𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻

𝗦𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝟭.𝟬 → 𝗦𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝟮.𝟬 → 𝗦𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝟯.𝟬

• 𝗦𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝟭.𝟬 (1940s):
You had to write every single rule by hand. Computers were dumb. Programmers were gods.

You told the machine exactly what to do, line by line.
Painfully rigid. Fully manual. No room for magic.

• 𝗦𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝟮.𝟬 (~2012):
Neural networks entered the chat.
Instead of writing rules, you trained the system by feeding it tons of data.

The machine learned patterns but only for fixed, like recognizing cats in photos. 
Smart, but not flexible.

• 𝗦𝗼𝗳𝘁𝘄𝗮𝗿𝗲 𝟯.𝟬 (~2019):
Comes LLMs, the programmable neural nets.
You don’t write rules. You don’t even tune weights.

You just talk to it.
𝐏𝐫𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐭𝐬 𝐛𝐞𝐜𝐚𝐦𝐞 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐧𝐞𝐰 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐠𝐫𝐚𝐦𝐦𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐥𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐮𝐚𝐠𝐞.
LLMs aren’t just tools, they’re operating systems.

𝐅𝐫𝐨𝐦 𝐜𝐨𝐝𝐞 → 𝐰𝐞𝐢𝐠𝐡𝐭𝐬 → 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐭𝐬.
We’ve moved from logic to learning to language.

And now, the best developers?
Aren’t just coders.
They’re 𝐩𝐫𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐭 𝐚𝐫𝐜𝐡𝐢𝐭𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐬, idea whisperers, system thinkers.

Welcome to the age of programmable intelligence.
Where thinking > typing.
𝐖𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞 𝘁𝗵𝗶𝗻𝗸𝗶𝗻𝗴 𝐢𝐬 𝐚 𝐬𝐤𝐢𝐥𝐥.

Over to you: Where software era did you started in?

json.content:
The cold email system that finally worked 👇🏻
 
In 2024, I struggled with cold emails.
 
I sent hundreds of emails, and guess what?
 
No replies.
 
Spam filters blocked most of them.
 
The ones that got through? Completely ignored.
 
I realized the problem wasn't just spam filters, it was that my entire approach was broken.
 
Here's how I turned things around:
 
1️⃣ Fixed my deliverability first
 
I stopped blasting emails blindly and started warming up my email accounts properly. With Saleshandy, I could connect unlimited email accounts and rotate between senders automatically. Their sequence score feature showed me exactly what was wrong with my emails before I even hit send.
 
2️⃣ Built campaigns that scale
 
Using Saleshandy's automated sequences, I created multi-step campaigns with personalized follow-ups. The sub-sequences feature let me move interested prospects into different flows automatically based on their replies. No more manual sorting.
 
3️⃣ Tracked everything that mattered
 
Saleshandy's unified inbox brought all replies from multiple accounts into one place. Their AI categorized responses automatically like positive, not interested, out of office. I could finally see what was working and double down on it.
 
The results?
 
- Open rates jumped from 15% to 45%
- Reply rates went from zero to 21%
- Closed 160+ leads for my clients
 
I could manage everything from one dashboard. No more juggling between tools or paying per inbox like other platforms charge.
 
Saleshandy changed how I think about cold outreach. It's not about sending more emails. It's about sending the right emails to the right people at the right time.
